blocks.text: If $z$ is a function of $x$ and $y$, i.e., $z = f(x, y)$, the total differential $dz$ is obtained from the partial differentials $dx$ and $dy$ by the use of the following rule:

blocks.description: This equation defines the total differential in terms of partial derivatives.

blocks.text: The reason for this is more clearly seen if we work from the fundamental idea of change, and introduce the actually measurable quantities like $x$, $y$, and $z$.

blocks.caption: Figure 21
blocks.description: The figure illustrates a point P on a surface defined by the function z = f(x, y), and shows how the position of P changes with respect to changes in x and y. The rate of change in z is represented as being proportional to the changes in x and y.

blocks.latex: \Delta z = change \ in \ z \ (\text{due to the change in } x) + change \ in \ z \ (\text{due to the change in } y)
blocks.description: This contributes to the total change in z when both x and y are varied.

blocks.text: Let P be a point $(x, y)$ on a surface, and let P move to a new position Q near to P on the surface (Q being kept constant). The change in z due to the change in x is measured by the product of the change in x multiplied by the rate at which z changes with respect to x; and this fact can be better illustrated by referring to a diagram (Fig. 21).
